claim: The grain sizes of the low-density steel samples after solution treatment range from 8.7 µm (S950) to 32 µm (S1150).
evidence_text: Low-density steels with various grain sizes (8.7–32 μm) were prepared by adjusting the solution treatment temperature between 950 ℃ and 1150 ℃. The coarsegrained sample (S1150), characterized by a grain size of 32 μm, forms a thinner oxide film…

claim: The oxide film on S1150 is thinner (18 ± 0.9 nm) and shows a reduction in Al content from 90.17 at% to 69.91 at% compared to S950.
evidence_text: the coarsegrained sample (S1150) … forms a thinner oxide film (18 ± 0.9 nm) with a reduced Al content (90.17 at% to 69.91 at%) and a higher defect density

claim: Prior to XPS and AES analysis, the air-formed oxide film was removed by cathodic polarization at −1.0 V_SCE for 300 s, then an oxide film was formed at −0.5 V_SCE for 7200 s.
evidence_text: the samples were cathodically polarized by applying − 1.0 V SCE for 300 s to remove the air-formed oxide film, and then the samples were polarized at − 0.5 V SCE for 7200 s to form the oxide film on the surface.

claim: AES depth profiling used a sputtering rate of 3.6 nm/min (relative to SiO2 on Si) and a signal collection area of 70 × 70 μm.
evidence_text: The sputtering rate was 3.6 nm/min (relative to the thermally oxidized SiO2 film on the surface of single crystal Si), and the collection area for the Auger electron signal was 70 × 70 μm.

claim: Electrochemical tests were conducted in deaerated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, with OCP measured for 3600 s, EIS from 10^5 to 0.01 Hz (10 mV excitation), potentiodynamic polarization at 0.3333 mV/s, and cyclic voltammetry from −1.2 to −0.3 V_SCE at 5 mV/s.
evidence_text: The open-circuit potential (OCP) was first measured for 3600 s. The frequency range of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was from 10^5 Hz to 0.01 Hz with an excitation voltage of 10 mV. The potentiodynamic polarization curve was tested by scanning from 0.4 V below the OCP to the anodic current density of 1 mA/cm^2 with a scan rate of 0.3333 mV/s. The scanning potential range of the cyclic voltammetry (CV) test was − 1.2 VSCE to − 0.3 VSCE, and the scanning rate was 5 mV/s.

claim: In previous studies on similarly heat-treated LDSs, the reported grain sizes varied from 9.6 μm to 50.2 μm and the corresponding pitting potentials ranged from −215 mV to −480 mV, indicating an unresolved grain size effect.
evidence_text: a comparison of these references reveals that the reported grain sizes differ significantly (9.6 μm-50.2 μm), and the corresponding pitting potentials also vary considerably (-215 mV~-480 mV) under similar heat treatment conditions.

claim: XRD analysis used Cu Kα radiation over a 30–90° 2θ range at a scan rate of 4°/min to investigate the crystal structure of the LDSs.
evidence_text: X-ray diffraction (XRD) … using Cu Kα radiation … The scanning range of the XRD … test was 30–90◦ with a scan rate of 4◦/min.

claim: Immersion corrosion testing was carried out in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 30 days with the solution changed every 7 days, and the corrosion rate was calculated from mass loss using Eq. (1).
evidence_text: The corrosion resistance of the LDSs was evaluated through immersion testing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. … The solution was replaced every 7 days, and the immersion period was 30 days. The corrosion rate was calculated by Eq. (1).

claim: With increasing solution treatment temperature from 950 °C to 1150 °C, the average grain size of LDSs increases gradually, reaching a maximum of 32.0 μm at 1150 °C, while the S0 as-rolled sample exhibits a nonuniform grain size distribution with large deformed and small fragmented grains.
evidence_text: The grain size of the S0 sample without solution treatment is uneven, featuring both large deformed grains and fragmented small grains resulting from thermal deformation during rolling (Fig. 2a). With increasing solution treatment temperature, the grain size gradually increases, as shown in Fig. 2b-d. … The maximum average grain size of 32.0 μm is achieved at the highest solution temperature of 1150 ℃.

claim: The S0 sample contains a substantial population of low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2–10°), while after solution treatment the S950, S1050, and S1150 samples exhibit complete recrystallization characterized by abundant annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, Σ3, 60°) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >10°) within equiaxed austenite grains.
evidence_text: The GB distribution analysis in Fig. 3a-d reveals that the S0 sample contains a substantial population of low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2–10°). After solution treatment, the S950, S1050, and S1150 samples exhibit complete recrystallization, characterized by abundant annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, Σ3, 60°) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >10°) within equiaxed austenite grains.

claim: The S950 sample has the maximum densities of both ATBs (0.146 μm/μm²) and HAGBs (0.114 μm/μm²), while the S1150 sample has the minimum densities of 0.040 μm/μm² and 0.019 μm/μm², respectively.
evidence_text: The S950 sample has the maximum densities of both ATBs and HAGBs, which are 0.146 and 0.114 μm/μm², respectively. These boundary densities progressively decrease with increasing solution temperature, with the S1150 sample reaching minimum values of 0.040 and 0.019 μm/μm², respectively.

claim: TEM images of the S0 sample reveal numerous κ‑carbides with diameters of 5–10 nm in the austenitic matrix and a high density of irregular dislocations; after solution treatment, dislocation density decreases and dislocation tangles appear adjacent to annealing twin boundaries.
evidence_text: The bright-field TEM image in Fig. 5a, along with the corresponding highresolution TEM (HRTEM) image and fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis (Fig. 5a), confirms the presence of numerous κ‑carbides (indicated by yellow arrows) with diameters ranging from 5 to 10 nm in the S0 sample. … the matrix of the LDSs is predominantly austenitic, and contains a high density of irregularly distributed dislocations in the S0 sample. As shown in Fig. 5b, the dislocation density decreases after solution treatment. Notably, dislocation tangles are observed adjacent to the ATBs, suggesting that the abundant ATBs effectively hinder dislocation movement.

claim: The S0 sample exhibits the highest breakdown current density i_bre of 9.92 μA/cm², the fine-grained S950 sample the lowest i_bre of 2.72 μA/cm², and the coarse-grained S1150 sample an i_bre of 8.84 μA/cm², indicating that finer grains improve corrosion resistance.
evidence_text: The S0 sample without solution treatment has the highest i_bre (9.92 μA/cm²), and is relatively more prone to corrosion. The i_bre of the fine-grained sample (S950) is 2.72 μA/cm², which is lower than that of the coarse-grained sample (S1150) at 8.84 μA/cm², and it has higher corrosion resistance.

claim: After potentiodynamic polarization, the S0 sample shows extensive corrosion damage with numerous deep pits, the S950 sample only slight pitting with smaller pits, and the S1050 and S1150 samples exhibit increased pit number and size.
evidence_text: According to Fig. 7a, the S0 sample exhibits extensive corrosion damage, characterized by numerous deep pits … After solution treatment at 950 °C, the LDS shows slight pitting corrosion (Fig. 7b), with smaller pit sizes, indicating superior corrosion resistance. However, as the grain size increases (Fig. 7c-d), the number and size of corrosion pits in the S1050 and S1150 samples increase.

claim: EIS fitting with an equivalent circuit having two time constants gives an Rp of 12486 Ω·cm² for S950 (approximately double S0's 6036 Ω·cm²), and Rp progressively decreases with increasing solution temperature.
evidence_text: Comparative analysis reveals that the S950 sample exhibits the highest Rp (12486 Ω·cm²), which is approximately double that of the S0 sample (6036 Ω·cm²). Additionally, the Rp values progressively decrease with increasing solution temperature.

claim: All LDSs display a single capacitive arc in Nyquist plots without Warburg impedance, indicating electrochemical reaction control; the S950 sample has the largest capacitive arc radius and thus the highest corrosion resistance, while S0 has the smallest radius.
evidence_text: The Nyquist plots in Fig. 8a reveals that all the samples display a single capacitive arc without Warburg impedance. This behavior suggests that the electrode reactions are predominantly controlled by electrochemical reactions. … The S0 sample without solution treatment displays the smallest capacitive arc radius, which demonstrates its greater susceptibility to corrosion. In contrast, the S950 sample has the largest capacitive arc radius, which indicates that it has the highest resistance and the lowest corrosion tendency.

claim: After 30-day immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the S950 sample exhibits the lowest corrosion rate of 0.160 mm/y, S0 the highest of 0.326 mm/y, and S1050 and S1150 show 0.248 mm/y and 0.281 mm/y, respectively; experimental variability is below 3%.
evidence_text: The S0 sample has the highest corrosion rate (0.326 mm/y), reflecting its inferior corrosion resistance. Solution treatment significantly reduces the corrosion rate, and the S950 sample exhibits the lowest corrosion rate at 0.160 mm/y. … The corrosion rates of the S1050 and S1150 samples are 0.248 mm/y and 0.281 mm/y, respectively. In addition, the experimental data show excellent reproducibility, with variations maintained below 3 % across multiple tests.

claim: During potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl, the S950 sample achieves the lowest stable current density of 2.45 µA/cm², outperforming S1050 (4.52 µA/cm²), S1150 (6.04 µA/cm²), and the S0 as-rolled sample (8.39 µA/cm²).
evidence_text: The S0 sample exhibits the highest stable current density (8.39 µA/cm²), which reflects relatively weak protection of its oxide film. With the increase of solution temperature, the grain size increases, and the stable current density of the LDSs gradually increases. The S950 sample achieves the lowest stable current density (2.45 µA/cm²), outperforming both S1050 (4.52 µA/cm²) and S1150 (6.04 µA/cm²).

claim: After 7200 s potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, EIS fitting (Table 3) yields the highest polarization resistance Rp of 21355 Ω·cm² for the S950 sample.
evidence_text: The S950 sample achieves the highest Rp of 21355 Ω·cm².

claim: The as-rolled S0 sample has the highest donor density ND of 0.7×10^21 cm⁻³ and acceptor density NA of 2.25×10^21 cm⁻³, indicating a highly defective oxide film.
evidence_text: The S0 sample exhibits the highest ND (0.7 × 10^21 /cm^3) and NA (2.25 × 10^21 /cm^3) values.

claim: The fine-grained S950 sample achieves the lowest donor density ND of 0.26×10^21 cm⁻³ and acceptor density NA of 0.68×10^21 cm⁻³, corresponding to fewer structural defects and superior oxide film compactness.
evidence_text: The S950 sample achieves the lowest ND (0.26 × 10^21 /cm^3) and NA (0.68 × 10^21 /cm^3) values.

claim: AES depth profiling shows the oxide film on S0 is 16.2 ± 0.8 nm, while S950 forms a significantly thicker film of 25.2 ± 1.2 nm predominantly composed of uniformly distributed Al oxides.
evidence_text: the oxide film thickness of S0 is approximately 16.2 ± 0.8 nm and consists mainly of Fe, Al, and O, with Fe and Al present in roughly comparable amounts. In comparison, the S950 sample has a significantly thicker film of 25.2 ± 1.2 nm, which is predominantly composed of uniformly distributed Al oxides.

claim: The oxide film thicknesses of S1050 and S1150 are 21.6 ± 1 nm and 18 ± 0.9 nm, respectively, confirming that oxide film thickness decreases with increasing grain size.
evidence_text: The thicknesses of the S1050 and S1150 oxide films are 21.6 ± 1 nm and 18 ± 0.9 nm, respectively.

claim: Mott-Schottky analysis reveals duplex semiconductor behavior: n-type in Region 1 (-1.13 to -0.93 VSCE) dominated by cation interstitials and oxygen vacancies, and p-type in Region 2 (-0.93 to -0.67 VSCE) dominated by cation vacancies.
evidence_text: In Region 1 (-1.13 VSCE to -0.93 VSCE), the positive slope confirms that the n-type behavior is dominated by cation interstitials and oxygen vacancies. The slope of the curve in Region 2 (-0.93 VSCE to -0.67 VSCE) shows negative slope characteristics indicative of p-type behavior, with cation vacancies as the primary defects.

claim: The oxide film thickness of S0 (as-rolled) determined by AES, where oxygen concentration decreases to half its maximum, is approximately 16.2 ± 0.8 nm.
evidence_text: the oxide film thickness of S0 is approximately 16.2 ± 0.8 nm and consists mainly of Fe, Al, and O, with Fe and Al present in roughly comparable amounts.

claim: The S950 sample has a significantly thicker oxide film of 25.2 ± 1.2 nm, which is predominantly composed of uniformly distributed Al oxides.
evidence_text: the S950 sample has a significantly thicker film of 25.2 ± 1.2 nm, which is predominantly composed of uniformly distributed Al oxides.

claim: The oxide film thickness of S1050 is 21.6 ± 1 nm.
evidence_text: The thicknesses of the S1050 and S1150 oxide films are 21.6 ± 1 nm and 18 ± 0.9 nm, respectively.

claim: The oxide film thickness of S1150 is 18 ± 0.9 nm.
evidence_text: The thicknesses of the S1050 and S1150 oxide films are 21.6 ± 1 nm and 18 ± 0.9 nm, respectively.

claim: The driving force for grain growth in low-density steels originates from the reduction of the system's free energy following grain boundary migration, which constitutes the primary mechanism for grain growth.
evidence_text: The driving force for grain growth originates from the reduction of the system's free energy following GB migration, which constitutes the primary mechanism for grain growth.

claim: Based on the classic grain growth model, the evolution of average grain size with temperature and time can be expressed by D^2 - D0^2 = kt, and k = k0 exp(-Q/RT), where D is the final grain size, D0 is the initial grain size, k is the diffusion coefficient, Q is the activation energy for diffusion, R is the gas constant, and T is the Kelvin temperature.
evidence_text: Based on the classic grain growth model, the evolution of average grain size with temperature and time can be expressed by Eqs. (8)-(9). D^2 - D0^2 = kt ... k = k0 exp(-Q/(RT))

claim: With increasing solution temperature, the diffusion coefficient k increases exponentially, which enhances atomic mobility and facilitates grain boundary migration, causing large grains to progressively swallow small grains and accelerating grain growth.
evidence_text: With increasing solution temperature, the diffusion coefficient k increases exponentially, which enhances the atomic mobility at elevated temperatures. This facilitates the migration of GB, where large grains progressively swallow small grains, thereby accelerating grain growth.

claim: High-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) possess higher energy and play a critical role in driving grain growth, while annealing twin boundaries (ATBs) are low-energy special boundaries that are gradually swallowed during accelerated grain boundary migration at higher temperatures.
evidence_text: GB energy plays a critical role in driving this process, particularly in the presence of HAGBs, which possess higher energy. ... Conversely, ATBs represent low-energy special boundaries, which are gradually swallowed due to accelerated GB migration at higher temperatures.

claim: The standard Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔG°) for Al₂O₃ is -756.3 kJ/mol, for FeO -116.7 kJ/mol, and for MnO -173.4 kJ/mol, indicating Al has the strongest thermodynamic driving force for oxidation.
evidence_text: The standard Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔG◦) values for the oxidation reactions of Al, Fe, and Mn are presented in Eqs. (10)-(12).

claim: Al resides within the stable zone of Al2O3 under environmental conditions, confirming its ability to spontaneously form a stable and dense oxide film even under anaerobic conditions; the main stable state of Fe is Fe2+ and FeO remains the main oxide, while Mn primarily exists as Mn2+ with a close stability region to MnO2.
evidence_text: The results reveal that Al resides within the stable zone of Al2O3 under environmental conditions, which confirms its ability to spontaneously form a stable and dense oxide film even under anaerobic conditions. The main stable state of Fe is Fe2+, although the thermodynamic stability zone of FeO in the E-pH diagram (Fig. 16b) is located in the high pH and negative potential region. ... FeO remains the main oxide of Fe, with minor quantities of Fe2O3 and FeOOH potentially forming at the film defects or localized highpotential regions.

claim: Without noticeable GB segregation, the oxide film formation is governed by the intrinsic structural characteristics of GBs: crystallographic misorientation causes atomic disorder at HAGBs, increasing interfacial free energy and enhancing susceptibility to anodic dissolution; the weaker binding energy and vacancy accumulation create short-circuit diffusion pathways, promoting dissolution activity and rapid nucleation and growth of oxide films.
evidence_text: the crystallographic misorientation between adjacent grains causes substantial atomic disorder at HAGBs, which generates structural defects that include lattice distortion, vacancy accumulation, and coordination deficiencies... thermodynamically increase the interfacial free energy at GBs to levels substantially higher than those in grain interiors, thereby enhancing their susceptibility to anodic dissolution... From a kinetic perspective, the weaker binding energy of boundary atoms decreases the activation energy required for metal ion dissolution... structural disorder coupled with vacancy accumulation creates short-circuit diffusion pathways, which significantly promote the dissolution activity of GBs in corrosive environments... GBs serve as continuous permeation networks... enabling rapid nucleation and growth of oxide films.

claim: Localized dissolution occurs at certain ATBs due to an approximately 30 mV potential difference between ATBs and the matrix, which establishes microgalvanic couples, induces metal dissolution extending along the twin boundary, but the uniform distribution of ATBs contributes to homogeneous oxide film formation.
evidence_text: localized dissolution occurs at certain ATBs, as shown in Fig. 17, which is attributable to the approximately 30 mV potential difference between ATBs and the matrix. ... this potential disparity facilitates microgalvanic couples at the interface of the ATBs and the matrix... induces metal dissolution extending along the twin boundary. The uniform distribution of ATBs contributes to maintaining the structural continuity and composition homogeneity of the oxide film

claim: Larger grain sizes with lower GB densities result in fewer reaction sites, slower film formation rate, a more discontinuous oxide layer, decreased Al content in the film, reduced thickness, and higher density of defects as shown by Mott-Schottky results (Fig. 12).
evidence_text: larger grain sizes correspond to lower GB densities, resulting in a sparsely distributed GB region that diminishes the number of reaction sites for oxide film formation ... results in a slower film formation rate and produces a more discontinuous oxide layer. ... an increasing grain size correlates with a decreased Al content within the oxide film, producing a thinner and compositionally heterogeneous film exhibiting higher density of defects (Mott-Schottky results, Fig. 12).

claim: In EIS immersion tests up to 168 h, the fine-grained S950 sample achieves an Rp of 114.9 kΩ·cm² at 168 h, while the coarse-grained S1150 sample reaches only 50.6 kΩ·cm²; S950 Rp increases rapidly in the first 2–8 h and stabilizes after about 24 h, indicating rapid formation of a dense and stable oxide film.
evidence_text: The fine-grained sample (S950) exhibits consistently higher Rp values throughout the entire immersion period, reaching 114.9 kΩ·cm2 at 168 h, which is significantly higher than that of the coarse-grained sample (S1150) at 50.6 kΩ·cm2. The Rp value of the S950 sample increases rapidly during the initial stage of immersion (2–8 h) and then stabilizes after approximately 24 h.

claim: In the first CV cycle, a distinct Al oxidation peak appears at approximately −0.81 to −0.77 V_SCE, with S950 showing the highest peak current; the charge density for Al oxidation is 2.55×10⁻⁵ C/cm² for S950, 1.80×10⁻⁵ C/cm² for S1050, and 1.66×10⁻⁵ C/cm² for S1150. No Fe oxidation peak near −0.56 V_SCE is observed in the first cycle because the rapidly formed Al-rich oxide film suppresses Fe oxidation.
evidence_text: the Al oxidation peak appears at approximately −0.81 V_SCE, with the S950 sample exhibiting the highest peak current density. ... the charge density of the S950 sample reaches 2.55 ×10⁻⁵ C/cm², which is higher than that of the S1050 (1.80×10⁻⁵ C/cm²) and S1150 (1.66×10⁻⁵ C/cm²). ... the characteristic Fe oxidation peak near −0.56 V_SCE is absent in the first cycle. This phenomenon arises because preferential oxidation and rapid film formation of Al coat the Fe surface with an Al-rich oxide layer, thereby restricting reaction sites and suppressing Fe oxidation

claim: In the second CV cycle, a Fe oxidation peak emerges at about −0.56 V_SCE; S950 shows the lowest peak intensity, indicating that a dense oxide film inhibits subsequent Fe and Mn oxidation, whereas S1050 and S1150 show enhanced anodic peaks due to incompletely formed film.
evidence_text: In the second cycle (Fig. 19b), a distinct Fe oxidation peak emerges at approximately −0.56 V alongside the persistent Al oxidation peak. The S950 sample displays the lowest peak intensity in this potential region. ... Conversely, the S1050 and S1150 samples demonstrate substantially enhanced anodic peaks at this position, suggesting that the surface film is incompletely formed

claim: Increasing solution temperature from 950 °C to 1150 °C increases the grain size from 8.7 μm to 32 μm and decreases the HAGBs density from 0.114 μm/μm² to 0.019 μm/μm².
evidence_text: The grain size increases progressively from 8.7 μm to 32 μm, while the HAGBs density decreases from 0.114 μm/μm² to 0.019 μm/μm².

claim: The S950 sample exhibits the lowest breakdown current density ibre of 2.72 μA/cm², the highest polarization resistance Rp of 12486 Ω·cm², and a corrosion rate of 0.160 mm/y, which is 43% lower than that of the S1150 coarse-grained sample.
evidence_text: The finegrained sample (S950) has the lowest ibre (2.72 μA/cm²) and the highest Rp (12486 Ω·cm²). Its corrosion rate is 0.160 mm/y, which is 43 % lower than that of the coarse-grained sample (S1150)

claim: The oxide film on LDSs exhibits duplex n-type and p-type semiconductor behavior; the acceptor density NA and donor density ND increase with grain size. For S950, NA is 0.68×10²¹ cm⁻³ and ND is 0.26×10²¹ cm⁻³, significantly lower than S1150. Grain coarsening reduces the Al content in the oxide film from 90.17 at% to 69.91 at% while increasing Fe and Mn contents.
evidence_text: Both the NA and ND values increase with increasing grain size. The NA and ND of the S950 sample are 0.68×10²¹ cm⁻³ and 0.26×10²¹ cm⁻³, respectively, which are significantly lower than those of the S1150 sample. ... grain coarsening reduces the Al content in the film from 90.17 at% to 69.91 at%, while increasing the Fe and Mn contents.

claim: HAGBs provide numerous nucleation sites for oxide film growth; fine grain size and high HAGB density promote rapid formation of a continuous, Al-rich, defect-poor oxide film during the initial corrosion stage, while coarse grains retard film growth, reduce Al content, and degrade film density and stability.
evidence_text: the fine-grained S950 sample has smaller grains and higher GB density, especially HAGBs and ATBs, facilitating the preferential dissolution of metallic elements (such as Al). ... HAGBs provide numerous nucleation sites for the growth of the oxide film. A continuous and stable Al-rich oxide film forms quickly during the initial stage of corrosion... Conversely, coarsegrained microstructures impede oxide film growth, reduce the Al content, and degrade the film density and stability.
